items.q: What is the reciprocal of the mixed number 2 1/3?
items.correct: 3/7 — first convert to the improper fraction 7/3, then flip it.
items.misconception: 1/2 1/3, by flipping each part of the mixed number separately.
items.why: Students don't convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before taking the reciprocal, and confuse 'reciprocal' with 'negative'.

items.q: What is the difference between simple and compound interest?
items.correct: Simple interest is calculated only on the original amount; compound interest is calculated on the original amount plus accumulated interest.
items.misconception: Simple and compound interest give the same total — compound just uses a different formula.
items.random_wrong: Simple interest grows faster than compound because it has fewer steps.
items.why: Students don't appreciate that compound interest reinvests each period's interest, leading to exponential rather than linear growth.

items.q: What is the derivative of a constant, like d/dx(5)?
items.correct: 0 — constants do not change with x.
items.misconception: The derivative of 5 is 5 because the constant stays the same.
items.random_wrong: The derivative of 5 is 1 because all constants differentiate to 1.
items.why: Students confuse 'value of a constant' with 'rate of change' — they don't see that 'no change' means derivative zero.
The answer examiners want is: π. “0.5” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: 9. “11” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 36. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
